summary: Jeff Atwood discusses how software projects fail when developers ignore fundamental practices, using Steve McConnell's paint can analogy from Rapid Development. The post opens with an example of a chaotic inherited codebase built by developers who each worked in isolation with wildly different approaches. McConnell's analogy compares software projects to painting jobs of varying scales: a doghouse can tolerate shortcuts, but a Boeing 747 demands strict adherence to instructions. Most software projects are 'house-sized' or larger, meaning cutting corners leads to significant technical debt. The core message is that mastering and following development fundamentals is what separates professional programmers from hobbyists.
key_points: Software projects commonly fail because developers don't follow established fundamentals, not because the problems are novel or difficult
The paint can analogy scales the consequences of ignoring instructions: doghouse (low stakes), house (medium), Boeing 747 (critical)
Knowing the fundamentals is the critical differentiator between hobby and professional programmers
Developers often skip best practices not due to conflicting visions but simply because 'they felt like doing something different'
Without mastering fundamentals, you lack project control and won't know if you're succeeding or failing until it's too late
money_quotes: They just felt like doing something different.
If you don't master the fundamentals first, you'll lack the project control needed to develop rapidly.
You won't even know whether you're succeeding or failing until late in the project.
Ignore the instructions if you wish, but do so at your peril.
Simply knowing the fundamentals is a critical differentiator between hobby programmers and so-called professional programmers.
You'll find the rank amateur; the pattern-a-holic; the global variable guy.
That's why The Daily WTF is more depressing than funny to me – we see the same classic mistakes day after day.
key_insight: Most software failures aren't caused by hard problems but by developers who skip the fundamentals—not because they have better ideas, but simply because they felt like doing something different.
